text: The video demonstrates a laparoscopic truncal vagotomy with sutured gastrojejunostomy and jejunostomy. Port placement is adjusted for suturing, with a 5mm port replacing the 12mm port for better ergonomics. The left lobe of the liver is retracted, and the gastrohepatic omentum is opened to access the right crus of the diaphragm. The frenoesophageal ligament is divided to develop the plane between the esophagus and the right crus. The retroesophageal space is entered and developed by blunt anterior dissection of the esophagus, revealing the thick posterior vagus nerve, which is divided using a bipolar or harmonic device. The anterior vagus nerve is exposed by baring the anterior and left lateral surface of the esophagus, dividing all nerve and connective tissue. The anterior vagus may appear as a single thick fiber, typically located anteriorly and to the left of the midline. After vagotomy, the esophagus is inspected for any missed vagal trunks.

The surgeon laparoscopically devascularizes the greater curvature of the stomach at the junction of the body and antrum, preparing the site for the gastrojejunostomy. The transverse mesocolon is lifted to identify the DJ flexure, and the jejunum is brought up for an anticholic anterior gastrojejunostomy. Two hitching stitches are placed at the corners of the stomach and jejunum to align them for the anastomosis.

The surgeon aligns the stomach and jejunum, creating a 4 cm enterotomy and gastrotomy on each loop. Using a Tuo PDS suture, the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy is completed with full-thickness bites of the jejunum and stomach. The anterior layer is sutured separately with extra mucosal stitches, ensuring mucosal inversion and serosal approximation. The camera port is positioned at the right subcostal region for optimal visualization during suturing.

The surgeon completes the jejuno-jejunostomy by aligning the jejunal loops with a trio silk stitch, followed by creating a 3 cm enterotomy on each loop. Using a Tuo PDS needle, the posterior layer is sutured with full-thickness bites, while the anterior layer is sutured with extra mucosal bites for serosal approximation and mucosal inversion. The ergonomic port placement facilitates the hand-sewn anastomosis, which is completed with a V-lock suture for the anterior seromuscular layer. The completed anastomosis is positioned 10 cm away from the gastro-jejunostomy to prevent efferent loop obstruction and bile reflux.

Basically in this technique the Truncal Vagotomy is done essentially in the same fashion as in the first video because the main incision and the main difficulty in exposure we need is for the Truncal Vagotomy. So the Truncal Vagotomy is done laparoscopically, the greater cover of the stomach is devascularized for the anastomosis, the mesocolon is lifted, the jejunal loop is identified and brought up to align with the stomach and the stomach and jejunum are being aligned here with a series of four seromuscular sutures. So till this point both the surgeries are same but once the stomach and the jejunum is aligned and these four seromuscular stitches are taken we actually do the anastomosis extracorporeally. So how we do it is basically at the completion of the fourth stitch we leave the thread very long, the needle holder which is there in the right hand working instrument is going to help hold this thread close to the anterior abdominal wall then we make a small mini laparotomy in this case it's a very tiny two centimeter laparotomy through which we deliver the medial edge of the gastrointestinal anastomosis and make a small gastrotomy and entrotomy and put a stapler through that and already the stomach is devascularized and they're aligned so we just fire the stapler and close the entrotomy and gastrotomy hand soon so this sort of main incision is required for the truncal vagotomy is avoided and the loops are aligned and just the small incision is used for the laparotomy in case you don't want to use a stapler rather than make a two centimeter incision you can make about a four centimeter incision and do the do the deliver the gj loop aligned gj loop through this incision and do the anastomosis extracorporeally even this way you'll be avoiding a much longer incision and the jj can also be done in an exactly similar fashion
